name: Apple III
desc: Apple's business computer, plagued by overheating and reliability problems after Apple chose silence over a cooling fan. The redesigned Apple III Plus arrived too late to save the line.

name: Apple Lisa
desc: Apple's first GUI-based business computer at $9,995. Technically remarkable, commercially ruinous, and quickly outclassed and outpriced by the Macintosh.

name: Apple QuickTake
desc: One of the first consumer digital cameras, made by Fujifilm and sold under Apple's name. Steve Jobs killed the entire product line within weeks of returning to Apple.

name: Pippin
desc: Apple's multimedia gaming platform co-developed with Bandai. It sold only about 42,000 units worldwide and cost nearly $600 at launch. An ambitious experiment in all the wrong directions.

name: Newton MessagePad
desc: Apple's pioneering PDA, famous for ambitious handwriting recognition that became a punchline. Steve Jobs canceled the Newton platform in 1998 after returning to Apple.

name: AirPort Base Station (Graphite)
desc: The original flying-saucer Wi-Fi base station Apple launched with the iBook, complete with a built-in 56K modem and 10BASE-T Ethernet. It helped kick-start mainstream Wi-Fi before Apple replaced it with a more capable second-generation model.

name: Power Mac G4 Cube
desc: A fanless, silent 8-inch cube of a Mac and a genuine design triumph. But at $1,799, with limited expandability and cheaper Power Mac G4 towers nearby, it was a commercial failure.

name: iTools
desc: Apple's first internet services bundle, offering a free @mac.com email address and iDisk cloud storage. Replaced by the paid .Mac subscription service.

name: Cinema Tools
desc: Apple's film-editing companion for Final Cut Pro, built for cut lists, change lists, and 24 fps workflows. It lasted barely a year as a standalone app before Apple folded it into Final Cut Pro 4.

name: AirPort Base Station (Dual Ethernet)
desc: Apple's second-generation flying-saucer Wi-Fi base station, with built-in firewall, dual Ethernet ports, and AOL support. It vanished when Apple jumped to 802.11g and renamed the line AirPort Extreme.

name: AirPort Card
desc: Apple's original 802.11b wireless card for the iBook and other AirPort-ready Macs. It helped drag Wi-Fi into the mainstream, then gave way to the smaller, faster AirPort Extreme Card with 802.11g.

name: HyperCard
desc: A revolutionary hypermedia and scripting environment that predated and inspired the World Wide Web. Quietly abandoned, then left to wither for a decade before being killed entirely.

name: iPod mini
desc: The colorful, compact iPod that made portable music fashionable. It quickly became a hit, then was discontinued to make way for the even thinner iPod nano.

name: Soundtrack
desc: Apple's loop-based royalty-free music tool for video editors, DVD authors, and web developers. It was later reinvented as the more ambitious Soundtrack Pro and disappeared as its own product.

name: Production Suite
desc: Apple's short-lived pre-Final Cut Studio bundle, combining Final Cut Pro HD, Motion, and DVD Studio Pro into a single pro-video suite. It was a transitional product and lasted less than a year.

name: Apple Wireless Mouse
desc: Apple's first Bluetooth mouse, launched alongside the first Apple Wireless Keyboard to cut cable clutter from Mac desks. It lasted until Apple replaced it with the more capable Wireless Mighty Mouse.

name: Xserve RAID
desc: Apple's 3U rack-mount RAID array for Xserve and pro media workflows. It offered unusually cheap high-capacity storage for its day, then disappeared as Apple backed away from enterprise storage hardware.

name: Shake
desc: Professional VFX compositing software used on major Hollywood films. Apple acquired it, sold it as a high-end Mac tool, then discontinued it in 2009 without a direct successor.

name: Final Cut Studio
desc: Apple's flagship pro-video suite bundling Final Cut Pro, Motion, Soundtrack Pro, DVD Studio Pro, and later Color and Compressor. It dominated mid-2000s Mac video production until Final Cut Pro X blew up the old suite model.

name: Apple Cinema Display
desc: Apple's widescreen external monitors, beloved by designers and video editors. The Cinema Display line was replaced by the Thunderbolt Display in 2011.

name: Final Cut Server
desc: A collaborative media asset management system for Final Cut Pro workflows in studios and broadcast. Discontinued in 2011 during Apple's messy Final Cut Pro X transition.

name: Front Row
desc: A full-screen media center app for Macs, designed to be controlled from the couch with the Apple Remote. Killed in OS X Lion without a replacement.

name: iDVD
desc: DVD menu and disc authoring software bundled with every Mac. Made beautiful animated menus, until optical drives quietly disappeared from Apple hardware forever.

name: iWeb
desc: Drag-and-drop personal website creator, tightly integrated with MobileMe hosting. As MobileMe was wound down and iCloud offered no web hosting replacement, iWeb was left behind.

name: Logic Express
desc: The affordable entry point to professional music production on Mac. Killed when Apple slashed Logic Pro 9's price to $199, eliminating the need for a budget tier.

name: Rosetta
desc: The translation layer that let PowerPC apps run on Intel Macs, enabling Apple's first major chip transition without breaking software. Removed in OS X Lion.

name: Xserve
desc: Apple's rack-mounted 1U server hardware and one of Apple's few genuine enterprise products. Discontinued as Apple pushed customers toward Mac Pro and Mac mini server configurations.

name: iTunes Ping
desc: Apple's music-focused social network. Steve Jobs personally called Mark Zuckerberg to negotiate a Facebook tie-in. Zuckerberg asked for too much; Ping launched anyway and flopped.

name: MobileMe
desc: Apple's cloud sync and email service. So unreliable at launch that Steve Jobs called it an embarrassment to the entire company at an internal meeting. Replaced by iCloud.

name: Aperture
desc: Apple's professional photo management and RAW editing suite, and Lightroom's closest rival for years. Killed in favor of Photos, which launched without many of Aperture's pro features.

name: iPod classic
desc: The hard-drive click-wheel iPod that defined the line for more than a decade. Discontinued in 2014 as Apple moved its music focus to flash storage, iPhone, and streaming.

name: Beats Music
desc: Dr. Dre and Jimmy Iovine's streaming service, praised for human-curated playlists. Apple acquired Beats for $3 billion and shut Beats Music down the next year as Apple Music took over.

name: iPhoto
desc: The photo management app bundled with every Mac for over 13 years. Replaced by Photos, a simpler, more iCloud-centric app that lost many of iPhoto's organizing features.

name: iAd
desc: Apple's mobile advertising platform. Steve Jobs said it would "reinvent" mobile advertising. It never seriously challenged Google, and Apple shut the network down after six quiet years.

name: iPhone Headphone Jack
desc: The 3.5mm audio port was present on every iPhone for 9 years. Apple called its removal "courageous" in 2016 and included a Lightning adapter in the box. The box no longer ships.

name: QuickTime for Windows
desc: Apple's media player for Windows, kept alive for over 20 years. Killed in 2016 with critical security vulnerabilities still unpatched. Users were advised to uninstall immediately.

name: Thunderbolt Display
desc: Apple's last standalone external monitor before the Pro Display XDR. Discontinued in 2016 without a direct Apple replacement, leaving pro Mac users waiting until 2019.

name: 32-bit iOS App Support
desc: iOS 11 dropped support for 32-bit apps, rendering thousands of older games and apps permanently unplayable on updated devices. Many developers had already moved on; many users had not.

name: iPod nano
desc: The ultra-thin, colorful iPod that evolved through seven generations and countless designs. A design icon of the 2000s and 2010s, killed alongside its sibling with no ceremony.

name: iPod shuffle
desc: The tiny clip-on music player with no screen, just shuffle. Simple, cheap, and genuinely fun. The only music player you could wear like a badge.

name: AirPort Express
desc: Apple's compact Wi-Fi router that doubled as a wired AirPlay audio receiver, beloved by people who wanted wireless audio without replacing their speakers. Gone with the rest of the AirPort line.

name: AirPort Extreme
desc: Apple's flagship home Wi-Fi router, later redesigned into a striking vertical tower. Discontinued as Apple exited the router business entirely.

name: Time Capsule
desc: A Wi-Fi router with a built-in hard drive for automatic, wireless Time Machine backups. An elegant hardware solution to an everyday problem, killed alongside the AirPort line.

name: iTunes
desc: The app that defined the digital music era, powered the iPod revolution, and built the App Store. After 18 years of bloat, replaced by Music, Podcasts, and TV apps in macOS Catalina.

name: MacBook (12-inch)
desc: Apple's thinnest, lightest laptop, with a single USB-C port, fanless design, and a beautiful Retina display. Too compromised for most buyers; too underpowered to justify its price.

name: 32-bit macOS App Support
desc: macOS Catalina dropped 32-bit app support entirely, breaking thousands of games and legacy applications after one major release of prominent warnings.

name: Texture
desc: A magazine subscription service born as Next Issue and later rebranded Texture, dubbed "the Netflix of magazines." Apple acquired it in 2018 and folded the idea into Apple News+.

name: Apple Remote
desc: Apple's minimalist infrared remote for Macs, iPod docks, and early Apple TVs, first in white plastic and later in aluminum. It faded out as Siri Remote and software remotes took over.

name: iTunes U
desc: Platform for universities, schools, and educators to share lectures and course materials through iTunes. Quietly shut down and partially absorbed into Apple Podcasts.

name: iPod touch
desc: The last surviving iPod, an iPhone without the cellular contract. Beloved by kids, travelers, and budget-conscious music fans for 15 years. The end of an era.

name: macOS Server
desc: Apple's server OS, once a serious enterprise product with directory services, file sharing, and device management. Wound down over years until it became just a few GUI wrappers.

name: HomePod (1st gen)
desc: Apple's premium smart speaker with genuinely excellent audio quality. Discontinued in 2021 as Apple focused on the cheaper HomePod mini, before reviving the full-size HomePod in 2023.

name: iPhone SE (3rd gen)
desc: Apple's last iPhone with a Home button, Touch ID, an LCD screen, and a Lightning port. Discontinued when Apple replaced the SE line with the iPhone 16e.

name: Macintosh TV
desc: A Mac LC 520 in a black case with a built-in TV tuner, and Apple's first attempt at merging computing and television. Only 10,000 units were ever made and it was never sold outside the US.

name: eMate 300
desc: A Newton-powered clamshell laptop built for students, with an integrated keyboard and weeks of battery life. Killed alongside the Newton when Steve Jobs returned.

name: AppleWorks
desc: Apple's bundled office suite, born as ClarisWorks in 1991. For 16 years it was the word processor and spreadsheet for millions of Mac and Apple II users. Retired when iWork arrived.

name: .Mac
desc: The paid successor to iTools: @mac.com email, iDisk cloud storage, and online sync for $99/year. Frequently unreliable. Rebranded as the even more unreliable MobileMe.

name: iSync
desc: A Mac utility for syncing contacts, calendars, and bookmarks across Macs, phones, and iPods over Bluetooth or USB. Removed from OS X Lion as iCloud made manual device sync feel obsolete.

name: eMac
desc: An education-focused all-in-one Mac built around a 17-inch CRT. Initially sold only to schools, briefly offered to consumers, then returned to education before being discontinued in 2006.

name: iSight
desc: Apple's sleek external FireWire webcam: aluminum, cylindrical, and perfectly designed. Discontinued once Apple started building cameras into iMacs, MacBooks, and displays.

name: 30-pin Dock Connector
desc: The wide 30-pin connector used on every iPod and iPhone for nine years. It spawned an entire accessories industry: alarm clocks, car adapters, speaker docks. Killed overnight by Lightning.

name: WebObjects
desc: A powerful web application server born at NeXT that Apple used to run the iTunes Store. Quietly deprecated in 2016 with no migration path for the enterprise customers who relied on it.

name: Game Center app
desc: Apple's standalone iOS app for achievements, leaderboards, and multiplayer matchmaking. The service survived, but the app was removed in iOS 10 after years of neglect.

name: Apple TV (3rd gen)
desc: The streaming set-top box that never got an App Store. It played video and little else, and lingered as the cheaper Apple TV until Apple finally discontinued it in 2016.

name: iTunes Radio
desc: Apple's free ad-supported streaming radio service, built into iTunes and iOS. A direct Pandora rival, folded into Apple Music within three years without so much as a goodbye.

name: Newsstand
desc: A dedicated iOS app folder for digital magazine and newspaper subscriptions. Publishers barely supported it; readers rarely opened it. Removed in iOS 9 and absorbed into Apple News.

name: 3D Touch
desc: Pressure-sensitive display tech enabling Peek, Pop, and shortcut menus based on press force. Quietly dropped from the iPhone XR in 2018, abandoned entirely with the iPhone 11.

name: Mac Pro (Trash Can)
desc: The radical cylindrical Mac Pro: striking to look at, thermally compromised inside. Apple went six years without an update and publicly admitted they had "painted themselves into a corner."

name: Apple TV Remote app
desc: Apple's official iPhone and iPad app for controlling Apple TV. Pulled from the App Store after Apple moved the remote into Control Center.

name: Dashboard
desc: macOS's dedicated layer of mini-apps called widgets, accessed with a keystroke. Groundbreaking in Tiger, disabled by default years later, and finally removed in macOS Catalina.

name: Network Utility
desc: A Mac app for network diagnostics: ping, traceroute, port scanning, Whois lookups. A staple for sysadmins and power users, deprecated in macOS Big Sur.

name: Touch Bar
desc: The OLED strip that replaced the function key row on MacBook Pros. A programmable, context-sensitive control surface that most users ignored, most developers never supported, and Apple finally dropped with the last 13-inch MacBook Pro.

name: Lightning Connector
desc: Apple's slim, reversible 8-pin connector, used on iPhone for more than a decade. Replaced on new iPhones by USB-C in 2023 after years of regulatory and industry pressure.

name: AirPower
desc: Apple's wireless charging mat, announced in 2017 to charge iPhone, AirPods, and Apple Watch at once from almost any position. Canceled in 2019 after Apple said it could not meet its own standards.

name: iMac G3
desc: The translucent Bondi Blue all-in-one that helped pull Apple back from the brink. Designed under Jony Ive, later offered in fruity colors, and proof that Apple could still ship something the world wanted.

name: iMac G4
desc: The sunflower iMac, with a flat-panel display floating on a chrome arm above a hemispherical dome. Beloved by designers and discontinued when Apple moved toward putting the whole computer behind the screen.

name: iBook
desc: Apple's consumer laptop line, born as the colorful G3 clamshell in tangerine and blueberry and later refined into the white iBook. Retired when Apple switched to Intel and replaced it with the MacBook.

name: Power Mac G5
desc: Apple's aluminum tower with a perforated cheese-grater face, promoted as the world's fastest personal computer. Steve Jobs promised 3GHz within a year. It never came, and Apple jumped to Intel two years later.

name: PowerBook
desc: Apple's professional laptop line for 15 years, evolving from the chunky 100 series to the titanium and aluminum G4. Retired when Apple switched to Intel and rebranded the line as MacBook Pro.

name: Hockey Puck Mouse
desc: The round USB mouse that shipped with the original iMac G3. Impossible to orient by feel and hated by every reviewer who touched it. Replaced within two years by the Apple Pro Mouse and remembered as a cautionary tale in industrial design.

name: Mighty Mouse
desc: Apple's first widely sold multi-button mouse, with a tiny clickable scroll ball on top. The ball clogged easily, leaving users rolling it upside down on paper to bring it back to life.

name: iPod Hi-Fi
desc: Apple's $349 boombox speaker dock for the iPod, personally championed by Steve Jobs at its launch as audiophile-quality. Discontinued 18 months later as the rest of the speaker industry caught up and sold the same thing for less.

name: Smart Battery Case
desc: Apple's official iPhone case with a built-in battery, instantly recognizable for the hunchback bulge that broke every other iPhone case design rule. Quietly discontinued in 2020 once MagSafe wireless charging arrived.

name: iPhone 5c
desc: The plastic, candy-colored iPhone marketed as the affordable option, sold alongside the premium 5s. It was outsold by the 5s in nearly every market and ended Apple's brief experiment with a mid-tier consumer iPhone.

name: iPhone X
desc: Apple's tenth-anniversary iPhone, the first with Face ID and an edge-to-edge OLED display. Discontinued exactly one year after launch, an unusually short lifespan for a flagship iPhone.

name: iPhone 13 mini
desc: The last genuinely small modern iPhone, beloved by a vocal minority and ignored by enough buyers that Apple killed the mini line in 2023.

name: Apple Watch (Series 0)
desc: The original Apple Watch, launched in 2015 amid breathless hype as the future of wearables. Sluggish and short on battery, it was retroactively nicknamed 'Series 0' once Series 1 arrived.

name: Sherlock
desc: The system search tool in Mac OS that hunted across local files, the web, and online databases. Replaced by Spotlight in 2005 and remembered as the origin of 'sherlocked', the verb for when Apple kills a third-party app by building its features in.

name: iChat
desc: Apple's instant messaging client, supporting AIM, Jabber, and Bonjour. The colorful chat bubbles became iconic before iChat was rebranded as Messages and folded into iMessage in OS X Mountain Lion.

name: Safari for Windows
desc: Apple's attempt to bring Safari to Windows, announced by Steve Jobs as part of his iPhone web strategy. It was quietly abandoned after Safari 5.1.7 in 2012, while Mac Safari continued on.

name: Mac OS 9
desc: The final version of the classic Mac OS, foundation of Apple computing for 18 years. Steve Jobs literally held a funeral for it on stage at WWDC 2002, complete with eulogy, organ music, and an open coffin.

name: OpenDoc
desc: Apple's ambitious component-based document framework, designed to replace monolithic apps with modular parts. Steve Jobs killed it soon after returning to Apple in 1997, calling it a distraction.

name: AppleTalk
desc: The networking protocol that let any Mac on a LocalTalk cable share files and printers with zero configuration. Phased out in macOS Snow Leopard after 24 years, as Apple standardized on TCP/IP and Bonjour.

name: iTunes Festival
desc: Apple's annual month-long free concert series at London's Roundhouse, featuring artists from Adele to Pharrell streamed live to iTunes users. Renamed Apple Music Festival in 2015 and quietly ended after the 2016 season.

name: Photo Stream
desc: Apple's free photo sync service that automatically pushed the last 1,000 photos to all your Apple devices for 30 days. Replaced by iCloud Photos and finally shut down in 2023 after running quietly in the background for 12 years.

name: FireWire
desc: Apple's high-speed serial bus for digital video and audio professionals. Faster than USB at launch, eventually beaten by cheaper USB and Thunderbolt, and gone from the last Mac with built-in FireWire by 2016.

name: MagSafe (laptop)
desc: The magnetic power connector that detached harmlessly when someone tripped over the cable, saving countless MacBooks from broken ports. Removed in 2016 for USB-C, then restored on MacBook Pro five years later.

name: eWorld
desc: Apple's cheerful AOL rival, built around a cartoon town of email, forums, news, and downloads. It was Mac-only, expensive, barely marketed, and shut down with only a fraction of AOL's audience.

name: Cyberdog
desc: Apple's OpenDoc-based internet suite with a web browser, email, news, FTP, and address book components. When OpenDoc was killed, Cyberdog was abandoned with it.

name: A/UX
desc: Apple's first official Unix operating system, blending UNIX System V with a familiar Macintosh interface. It gave Apple a credible enterprise Unix story years before Mac OS X, then vanished without a successor.

name: Classic Environment
desc: The compatibility layer that let Mac OS 9 apps run inside early Mac OS X. It kept old software alive through the transition, then disappeared in Mac OS X Leopard.

name: iWork.com
desc: Apple's web-based document sharing and commenting service for Pages, Numbers, and Keynote. It never escaped beta, then was shut down as Apple pushed users toward iCloud.

name: Cards
desc: An iOS app for designing physical letterpress greeting cards that Apple would print and mail for you. Charming, oddly tactile, and dead less than two years later.

name: iBooks Author
desc: Apple's Mac app for creating interactive ebooks and textbooks. It once looked like the future of digital publishing, then Apple folded its core features into Pages and pulled the app for new users.

name: Clips
desc: Apple's playful short-form video editor for iPhone and iPad, with captions, filters, stickers, posters, and effects. It outlived several social-video trends before Apple finally stopped updates and downloads.

name: Apple Music Voice Plan
desc: A cheaper Siri-only Apple Music subscription that removed the normal app interface from streaming. Apple killed it after less than two years, presumably because yelling song requests at Siri was not the future.

name: iMovie Theater
desc: iMovie's iCloud-backed showcase for shared clips, movies, and trailers across Mac, iPhone, iPad, and Apple TV. Apple eventually dropped support and told users to move everything into iCloud Photos instead.

name: Apple Pay Later
desc: Apple's own buy-now-pay-later service, letting users split purchases into four payments. Barely a year after launch, Apple abandoned the in-house version and shifted toward third-party installment providers.

name: Back to My Mac
desc: A remote access feature that used iCloud to find and connect to your Macs over the internet. Magical when it worked, mysterious when it didn't, and gone from all macOS versions in 2019.

name: Apple SIM
desc: A carrier-switching SIM system for cellular iPads, designed to make mobile data plans less tied to one network. Eventually made redundant by eSIM, and new activations ended in 2022.

name: Dark Sky
desc: The beloved hyperlocal weather app famous for minute-by-minute rain alerts. Apple acquired it, shut down the Android app and API, then folded its best ideas into Apple Weather.

name: Lala.com
desc: A cloud music service where users could stream their libraries and buy cheap web-only songs. Apple acquired it in 2009 and shut it down months later, years before Apple Music arrived.

name: HopStop
desc: A transit directions app covering hundreds of cities before Apple Maps had serious public transport features. Apple bought it, used the knowledge, then shut it down as Maps gained transit directions.

name: Chomp
desc: An app search and discovery engine that helped users find apps by what they did, not just what they were called. Apple acquired it to improve App Store search, then shut the service off.

name: Find My Friends
desc: Apple's standalone location-sharing app for keeping track of friends and family. Merged into the unified Find My app in iOS 13, alongside Find My iPhone.

name: Final Cut Express
desc: Apple's semi-pro video editor for people who wanted more than iMovie but less than full Final Cut Pro. Discontinued during the Final Cut Pro X reset.

name: Soundtrack Pro
desc: Apple's professional audio editing and sound design app for video editors. Bundled with Final Cut Studio, then killed when Apple collapsed the old pro video suite.

name: DVD Studio Pro
desc: High-end DVD authoring software for creating professional menus, masters, and replicated discs. Essential in the DVD era, stranded when Apple decided optical media was over.

name: Color
desc: A professional color-grading app born from Apple's acquisition of Silicon Color. It brought serious finishing tools to Final Cut Studio, then disappeared in the Final Cut Pro X transition.

name: iMac Pro
desc: The Space Gray all-in-one workstation Apple built as a stopgap for professionals waiting on a real Mac Pro. Expensive, powerful, barely updated, and eventually replaced in spirit by Mac Studio.

name: 27-inch iMac
desc: The big-screen iMac that defined desktop Macs for over a decade. Apple killed it after launching Mac Studio and Studio Display, leaving the 24-inch iMac as the only all-in-one Mac.

name: Mac Pro
desc: Apple's tower workstation line for professionals who needed maximum expansion and maximum invoices. After years of shrinking relevance beside Mac Studio, Apple finally discontinued the line entirely.

name: MacBook Air (11-inch)
desc: The tiny wedge-shaped MacBook Air that felt like a netbook from a better universe. Loved by travelers, students, and writers, then axed as Apple pushed larger Retina notebooks.

name: MacBook Pro (17-inch)
desc: Apple's biggest professional notebook, beloved by editors, developers, and anyone who wanted a portable desktop. Killed when Retina MacBook Pros arrived and Apple decided 15 inches was enough.

name: Apple Watch Edition (gold)
desc: The original luxury Apple Watch made from 18-karat gold and sold for five-figure prices. Apple quietly retreated from the jewelry-store strategy and replaced it with ceramic.

name: MagSafe Battery Pack
desc: Apple's magnetic battery pack for iPhone 12 and later, elegant but small and slow compared with third-party alternatives. Discontinued when the iPhone moved from Lightning to USB-C.

name: MagSafe Duo Charger
desc: Apple's folding travel charger for iPhone and Apple Watch. Clever, overpriced, and discontinued alongside the MagSafe Battery Pack after the USB-C iPhone transition.

name: Apple USB SuperDrive
desc: The external CD and DVD drive that survived long after Apple removed optical drives from Macs. After 16 years, it quietly went out of stock worldwide and effectively disappeared.

name: Apple Network Server
desc: A short-lived PowerPC server line that did not even run Mac OS, shipping instead with IBM AIX. Expensive, strange, and canceled during Apple's 1997 product purge.

name: Apple II line
desc: The 8-bit computer family that made Apple a real company and dominated classrooms for years. It outlived the Apple III, Lisa, and many early Macs before Apple finally ended the line in 1993.

name: LaserWriter line
desc: Apple's PostScript laser printer family, a key piece of the desktop publishing revolution. Once essential to the Mac ecosystem, it was killed when Steve Jobs cut non-core peripherals.
